cartographie-des-affinites-democratiser-.english: # Affinity mapping: Democratizing the visualization of data Nowadays, many of our actions are transformed into digital information, which we can use to draw diagrams that describe complex functions, such as those of institutions. Data visualization allows us to unravel this complexity. The book introduces us to the reading of complex systems through the concept of affinity: the alchemy that brings people together and makes them creative and productive. Affinity mapping is a method of visualizing data that allows us to observe the dynamics of an organization subdivided into complex systems: institutions, universities, governments, etc. It is a graphic tool based on the variable of collaboration. Mapping affinities is, according to the author, an instrument for deciphering complex organizations and improving them, but also, through the insertion of individuals on these maps, a way of helping them to understand how to evolve in life within an institution. The book deals with this problem with a case of study concerning the School of Polytechnics of the Great-Neighborings of the Federal-Neighbouring Organizations. > > — Manuel Lima > The "diagrammatic" mapping designed by Dario Rodighiero combines the complexity of an esoteric vision with the clarity of logic. This well presented approach promises to give us the means to "read" huge amounts of recent data so that this statistical richness becomes graphically legible. > > > In the process followed, the transformation of simple information into schematic visualisations allows us to show latent, real and potential affinities in an approach that resembles digital alchemy. It is a fascinating work and full of possibilities. > > — Johanna Drucker ## Preface The heart of Dario Rodighiero's book, entitled _Cartography of affinities, is a case study: that of a very sharp project aimed at mapping the ongoing research at EPFL, the Federal Polytechnic School of Lausanne, Switzerland. However, this case study is much more than a case study. It raises vast questions about research, on organizational structures, on the approach taken, the social dynamics, on the basis of the process of social dynamics, on the perspectives, on the career path of the universities. This approach is based on the careful, critical and creative examination of the datasets, on the other hand, on the understanding of visualization as a complex and interactive form of narrative and, finally, on the consideration of the different audiences and the modes of engagement that a visualization of data must strive to reconcile. The result here is an extremely convincing research report on a collective mapping, which is intended to be less as a product than as a process whose successes and failures must be studied as a potential means of engaging the community in question – and the outside public – in the same process. The already old systems of scientometrics and bibliometrics, whose use is trivialized in universities in recent decades – with scientific citation indices (SCI), h-index and impact factor calculations (FI) – are undoubtedly appropriate to describe the emerging part of the liceberg called "research": published accounts, citations and autorats, grant allocation and composition of scientific citations (SCI), h-index and "impact factor calculations" (FI) – which are undoubtedly suitable to describe the "record" part of published accounts, quotes and autorats, and composition of research teams, their ownings. Scientometrics and bibliometrics teach us little about the form or context of a scientific community, about its network of connections with other groups of internal or external researchers, or about the balance between the roles played by individuals and those of the research units to which they belong. Moreover, because of their limited concern for publications, these systems tend, by nature, to be retrospective and thus neglect any mode of informal interaction of research that is not of the order of the published. How to describe research or collaborations not "after the fact", but as nascent or potential structures (i.e., from the perspective of predictive results, recommended)? And how to dig under the superficial layers of networks of autorat and quotes to reach the successive strata of forms of knowledge and communities that animate them? Rodighiero intervenes at this point precisely, focusing on networks based on affinities rather than on influence, the impact or collaboration of complex networks of autorat and of citations to reach the successive strata of forms of knowledge and the communities that animate them? Rodighiero intervenes at this precisely, rather than on the networks based on the influence, impact or collaboration of complex communities with formality. The designers are challenged on several fronts: they must feed and improve the conventional sets of institutional data in order to make them sufficiently rich and complex; they must then shape this data into objects of experiments with meaning for various audiences, ranging from the direction of a university to its researchers and students through the outside community. And to achieve these objectives, they must present these experiences in multiple ways: online or on-site, spatialized or incorporated. In the end, the democratization of the data is accomplished by design, and not only by the transparency with which the data are designed, analyzed and disseminated. — Jeffrey Schnapp _Historian, designer and technologist; founder and director of metaLAB (at) Harvard; co-director of the Berkman Center for Internet and Society; professor of Romanesque languages and literature and comparative literature, and affiliated with the Faculty of Architecture, Harvard University._ # Introduction Computer sociology aims to unravel the network of daily practices by analyzing digital traces that members leave on the cloud. Knowledge of how employees work is fundamental to making decisions and planning for the future. In particular, managers want to be able to have a global perspective in order to optimize the performance of their employees. The concept of performance here refers to the ambition and challenge of getting the best of an organization. To evaluate this performance, the management of a structure often uses indicators. Today, there are two interesting phenomena: the first is that the representation of these indicators is increasingly changing from a tabular form to a graphic form; the second, that these same indicators are available to all, implying both a form of transparency and the possibility of self-examination. Nowadays, performance is not only of interest to companies but also to universities. Academics are often evaluated in terms of their publications using the h-index or impact factor. Directors use these indicators to recruit academics and, in turn, they try to improve them to be "good". This bi-directional use confirms that the academic world adheres to a variety of themes that are related to the character of the subject matter. Affinities can also be classified into two categories, real and potential. The presence of a number of potential affinities indicates that there is a probable and predictable link between researchers: common interests, interdisciplinarity, intellectual culture, professional career or participation in journals, conferences or scientific committees. Potential affinities become real from the moment a collaboration takes place; this may be the case in the co-authorate of an article or in the follow-up of the same doctoral student. Therefore, affinities have two different dimensions: one is concrete and composed of ongoing collaborations, the other is projected and made of opportunities for exchange and prospection in the academic environment. Measuring affinities is essential for academic structures. And translating them into visual representation allows a space to be created where actual and potential performances can be combined. Unlike other systems of measurement, the other is projected and made of opportunities for exchange and prospection in the academic environment. Measuring affinities is essential for academic structures. The measurement of affinities and its graphic representation must therefore be at the disposal of all this collective in order to visually guide individuals in their personal choices. This book is the result of five years of work at the École polytechnique fédérale de Lausanne (EPFL) during which the affinities mapping project was carried out by an approach based on graphic design, i.e. by visual means, which are the only solution to manage the enormous mass of data that humans produce exponentially. The innovation of this work lies less in the question itself than in the attempt to reconcile the human sciences and technology in a multidisciplinary approach evoking the spirit of a contemporary Bauhaus.
